method3.generated_article:
==Volcano and caldera==
The Hunga volcano is a mostly submarine stratovolcano in the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec intra-oceanic arc, formed where the Pacific Plate subducts beneath the Indo-Australian Plate.[4] The volcanic edifice rises more than 1.8 km from the seafloor and extends about 20 km across, with a large summit caldera roughly 5 km in diameter.[4] Multibeam sonar surveys identified a 4 km by 2 km, 150 m deep depression at the inferred caldera site, consistent with collapse during an earlier explosive eruption.[2]

Before 2022, only two small parts of the caldera rim emerged above sea level as the uninhabited islands of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai.[4] Earlier geological work described the two islands as elongated features tangent to a circle around a rocky shoal south of Hunga Tonga, leading researchers to interpret them as the northern and western remnants of a large submarine caldera rim.[2] Rock samples from Hunga Tonga showed alternating layers of andesitic lava flows, scoria, lapilli and ash dipping gently away from the caldera centre.[2] The volcano’s eruptive products are andesitic, a composition common in intra-oceanic volcanic arcs.[4]

The 2014–2015 eruption built a large tephra cone that connected Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai into a single island known as Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai.[4][5] The newly formed island attracted scientific interest because its rapid erosion and landform evolution provided an analogue for interpreting similar features on Mars.[4] By late 2021, the joined island remained broadly similar to its post-2015 form despite shoreline erosion and settling.[2]

The 15 January 2022 eruption destroyed most of the joined island and left only small remnants of the former Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai islands above sea level.[4][5] The event has been described as a once-in-1,000-years eruption for the Hunga caldera and may rank as high as VEI 5, although further analysis is required to confirm the erupted volume.[5] The eruption produced the largest underwater explosion recorded by modern scientific instruments and generated shock waves through the ground, ocean and atmosphere.[1][3]

==Geography==
The site is in the central Tongan island chain, about 30 km south of Fonuafoʻou and 65 km north of Tongatapu, Tonga’s main island.[4]  
Regionally, it lies within the Tonga–Kermadec subduction system, which extends for about 2,550 km between New Zealand and Tonga.[5]  
Before the 2022 eruption, the above-water land formed a compact island of just over 1 km², with a maximum elevation of 149 m.[4]  
After the eruption, the former island’s exposed geography was reduced to very small remnants at its western and eastern margins.[4]  
The short-lived island was notable for satellite-based observation of rapid landform change, with studies of its erosion considered useful for interpreting similar volcanic features on Mars.[5]

==Eruptions==
The major 2022 eruptive phase began on 15 January at about 5 p.m. local time, when the shallow submarine vent erupted violently and generated a plume that reached the stratosphere.[1][4] Satellite-era observations recorded a vertical plume more than 30 km high, with overshooting tops above 55 km, making it one of the highest volcanic plumes observed by satellite.[5] Estimates from surface-pressure data placed the energy release of the initial explosion at 10–28 exajoules, comparable to or greater than several historic large eruptions.[5]

The eruption was strongly influenced by interaction between seawater and magma, with the vent lying about 200 m below the ocean surface.[1] Researchers described the event as more explosive than a typical Surtseyan eruption because very large volumes of seawater appear to have entered the hot magmatic system.[3] One proposed mechanism was partial collapse of the caldera rim, which may have allowed seawater to rush into an underground magma chamber and flash rapidly to steam.[3]

The eruption triggered tsunamis that struck Tonga and other Pacific coastlines.[4] In Tonga, run-up reached as high as 65 ft above sea level, destroying hundreds of structures, displacing more than 1,500 people and causing four deaths.[4] Tsunami impacts were also reported around the Pacific, including two deaths and an oil spill in Peru, as well as damage in Fiji, Hawaii, Chile, California, Japan, Russia and New Zealand.[4]

The eruption injected both volcanic aerosols and an exceptional amount of water vapor into the stratosphere.[2] The water vapor increased total stratospheric water content by about 10%, an unusual effect for a volcanic eruption because most major eruptions are dominated climatically by sulfur aerosols.[2] Sulfur dioxide from the eruption was converted into sulfate aerosols, which reflect sunlight and tend to cool the atmosphere.[1] Subsequent studies found that the eruption produced a slight net cooling effect from 2022, including an estimated 0.1 °C cooling over the Southern Hemisphere, rather than the warming that some researchers initially expected from the added water vapor.[1]

The lack of nearby seismic instruments complicated real-time interpretation of the eruption and its aftermath.[4] At the time of the event, the nearest functioning seismic station was almost 500 mi away, so researchers at the USGS National Earthquake Information Center developed methods to detect earthquakes that automated systems had missed.[4] Post-eruption marine surveys found fine sandy mud, deep ash ripples, gouged valleys and large sediment piles up to 50 km from the volcano.[4] Tonga’s domestic internet cable was severed during the eruption and later found buried beneath about 100 ft of sediment and ash.[4]

==Footnotes==
* Scientific literature commonly abbreviates Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai as HTHH and identifies it as an underwater volcano in the Tonga–Kermadec volcanic arc at about 20.536°S, 175.382°W, roughly 150 m below sea level.[1]
* The 15 January 2022 eruption has been described as one of the largest volcanic events recorded with modern geophysical instruments, with an estimated volcanic explosivity index of about 6, a plume reaching up to 57 km altitude, and tephra dispersal across an area at least 600 km in diameter.[1]
* Contemporary scientific and news accounts described the event as the largest recorded volcanic eruption in more than three decades, with tsunami effects observed across the Pacific, including in Tonga, Japan, Australia, and the west coasts of North and South America.[2][3]
* One proposed explanation for the eruption’s explosiveness is that a submarine landslide removed overlying rock, allowing the volcano to explode violently; island volcanoes are considered especially unstable and prone to such collapse.[2]
* The eruption prompted renewed study of Pacific tsunami hazards because the resulting waves involved both volcanic and atmospheric–oceanographic processes, including resonance effects that complicated conventional tsunami expectations.[3]
* Later climate studies found that the eruption injected exceptional amounts of water vapor into the stratosphere, producing cooling in the mid-stratosphere for more than a year while warming parts of the lower stratosphere.[4]
* A UCLA-led analysis reported that, despite expectations of warming from stratospheric water vapor, the eruption produced net cooling of about 0.1 °C in the Southern Hemisphere by the end of 2022 and 2023, partly because sulfate aerosols and ozone changes altered radiative effects.[5]

The Hunga volcano is a mostly submarine stratovolcano in the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec intra-oceanic arc, formed where the Pacific Plate subducts beneath the Indo-Australian Plate.[4] The volcanic edifice rises more than 1.8 km from the seafloor and extends about 20 km across, with a large summit caldera roughly 5 km in diameter.[4] Multibeam sonar surveys identified a 4 km by 2 km, 150 m deep depression at the inferred caldera site, consistent with collapse during an earlier explosive eruption.[2]

The 15 January 2022 eruption destroyed most of the joined island and left only small remnants of the former Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai islands above sea level.[4][5] The event has been described as a once-in-1,000-years eruption for the Hunga caldera and may rank as high as VEI 5, although further analysis is required to confirm the erupted volume.[5] The eruption produced the largest underwater explosion recorded by modern scientific instruments and generated shock waves through the ground, ocean and atmosphere.[1][3]

The site is in the central Tongan island chain, about 30 km south of Fonuafoʻou and 65 km north of Tongatapu, Tonga’s main island.[4]

* The 15 January 2022 eruption has been described as one of the largest volcanic events recorded with modern geophysical instruments, with an estimated volcanic explosivity index of about 6, a plume reaching up to 57 km altitude, and tephra dispersal across an area at least 600 km in diameter.[1]
